title: Postpositions
situation: Navigate space, time, and relationships — Hindi's version of prepositions (but they come AFTER)

phrases.english: in the house / in the office
phrases.context: 'mein' = in/inside. THE most common postposition. Comes AFTER the noun, unlike English prepositions

phrases.english: on the table / on the roof
phrases.context: 'pe' (colloquial) or 'par' (formal) = on/at. 'pe' is what you'll hear in conversation 90% of the time

phrases.english: I have a phone (near me / in my possession)
phrases.context: 'ke paas' = near/with/having. Hindi has NO verb 'to have' — you say 'near me [thing] is'. Mind-bending but essential

phrases.hindi: store ke saamne / ghar ke peeche
phrases.english: in front of the store / behind the house
phrases.context: 'ke saamne' = in front of, 'ke peeche' = behind. Two-word postpositions always use 'ke' as connector
phrases.pronunciation: STORE ke SAAM-ne / GHAR ke PEE-chhe

phrases.hindi: do baje ke baad / khaane se pehle
phrases.context: 'ke baad' = after, 'se pehle' = before. Note: 'before' uses 'se' not 'ke'. Time expressions need these
phrases.pronunciation: DO ba-JE ke BAAD / KHA-ne se PEH-le

phrases.context: 'se' = from, 'tak' = until/to. Together they create from→to. 'ghar se office tak' = from home to office
'ke liye' = for (purpose/benefit). With pronouns: 'mere liye' (for me), 'uske liye' (for him/her)

phrases.english: by bus / by auto / on foot
phrases.context: 'mein' for enclosed vehicles (bus/train/car), 'se' for open vehicles (auto/bike) or means. 'paidal' = walking
'ke baare mein' = about (topic). Literally 'in the matter of'. Very common in conversation
'ke bina' = without, 'ke saath' = with (accompanying). 'mere saath chal' = come with me

grammar_notes: POSTPOSITIONS come AFTER the noun (unlike English prepositions). English: 'in the house', Hindi: 'ghar mein' (house-in). Always noun + postposition
Simple postpositions: mein (in), pe/par (on/at), se (from/by/with), tak (until/to), ko (to/object marker)
Compound postpositions use 'ke/ki' as connector: ke paas (near), ke saamne (in front), ke peeche (behind), ke upar (above), ke neeche (below), ke baad (after), ke liye (for)
The connector changes with gender: 'ke' (masculine), 'ki' (feminine). 'Ghar ke saamne' but 'dukaan ki taraf' (toward the shop-feminine)
Pronouns change form before postpositions: main→mere/mujh, tu→tere/tujh, woh→uske/us, hum→hamare/hum. This is called the oblique case
culture_notes: Hindi has NO verb 'to have'. Possession is expressed as 'near me [thing] is': 'mere paas paise hain' = I have money (literally: near-me money is)
Directions in India use landmarks, not street names: 'mandir ke saamne' (in front of the temple), 'nadi ke us paar' (across the river)

skill_breakdown.explanation: Hindi uses postpositions (after the noun) instead of prepositions. 'ko' = to/for (direct/indirect object), 'se' = from/with/by, 'mein' = in/inside, 'par' = on/at.

more_examples.english: the book is on the table

skill_breakdown.explanation: Hindi possessive agrees with the OBJECT possessed, not the owner. 'mera bhai' (my brother, masc) vs 'meri behen' (my sister, fem). 'X ka' (masc), 'X ki' (fem), 'X ke' (masc-plural / before postposition).

more_examples.hindi: Rahul ka ghar bahut bada hai

skill_breakdown.skill: Compound postpositions (ke baare mein, ke saath, ke liye)
skill_breakdown.explanation: Combine 'ke' with another word: 'ke baare mein' (about), 'ke saath' (with), 'ke liye' (for), 'ke paas' (near / in possession of), 'ke piche' (behind). Always uses oblique 'ke' regardless of gender.

more_examples.hindi: aapke baare mein bata rahe the

more_examples.english: I don't have money (lit: near me money is not)

practice_prompt: Practice postpositions by describing where things are, giving directions, or talking about time. Tell me where you live, how you get to work, or what's near your house. I'll help you get the word order right.
